summary: The post examines the limitations of crowdfunding as a funding model, sparked by debate over Marco Arment's decision to fund his podcast app Overcast 2 through patronage. Asparouhova argues that crowdfunding is essentially a marketing campaign, which disadvantages skilled creators who lack strong personal brands. She frames crowdfunding as an evolutionary step in financing—just as AngelList syndicates evolved venture capital and Kickstarter evolved friends-and-family rounds—and asks what the next evolution beyond crowdfunding might look like. The core tension she identifies is that society has validated a desire to fund creativity, but the current mechanism rewards self-promotion over creative merit. She points to AngelList Syndicates as a model that aggregates crowd capital while simplifying the experience for recipients, and asks what an equivalent would look like for creative crowdfunding.
key_points: Crowdfunding is fundamentally a marketing campaign, requiring creators to solicit many small checks rather than one large one, which favors those with strong personal brands
The debate around Overcast 2's patronage model reveals an uncomfortable tension: patronage works for people with established brands but leaves most creators behind
Crowdfunding validates society's desire to see creativity exist in the world, but the marketing-heavy nature of platforms like Kickstarter is a bug, not a feature
Many of the most interesting creators are poorly disposed to personal brand building, because the most interesting ideas are often the most unpopular
AngelList Syndicates offer a model for crowdfunding's next evolution: aggregating crowd capital while presenting a single check to the recipient
money_quotes: One of the biggest limitations of crowdfunding is that it is essentially a marketing campaign.
If we acknowledge that creative contributions are valuable to society, the marketing-heavy nature of running a successful Kickstarter campaign starts to look more like a bug than a feature.
Surely we can agree that not every skilled creator has a strong personal brand, nor cares to build one.
Some of the most interesting ideas are also the most unpopular.
Crowdfunding validates our desire to see creativity exist in the world.
AngelList syndicates are the next evolution of venture capital. Kickstarter is the next evolution of friends-and-family rounds (or having a rich uncle).
key_insight: Crowdfunding has validated society's desire to fund creativity, but its reliance on personal brand and marketing means the next evolution must find ways to aggregate crowd capital without requiring creators to be self-promoters.
